headline: Germany’s Late Equalizer Revives Its World Cup Hopes
byline: By Weiyi Cai, Chloé Desaulles, Alexandre Devaux, Or Fleisher, Allison McCann, Mark McKeague, Scott Reinhard and Bedel Saget — Nov. 27, 2022

beats.text: AL KHOR, Qatar — Germany didn’t beat Spain, but a tie was enough to keep its World Cup hopes alive. After conceding a goal in the second half, Germany looked to be on the path to elimination. But in the 83rd minute Lukas Klostermann picked off an errant pass from a Spanish defender and took off running down the right wing.
He blew past Pedri, one of the best players in the world, and slid a short pass inside to Leroy Sané.
Sané received the ball on the turn, quickly cutting toward the center of the field and attracting three Spanish players.
Just ahead of Sané, Jamal Musiala started to make a diagonal run across the top of the box, drawing both of Spain’s central defenders toward him.
Sané played a through ball to Musiala, splitting the Spanish defense.
Sané’s pass was a bit behind Musiala and it looked as though he might lose the ball. But he kept the play alive with the deft touch of his right foot, flicking it past Spain’s last defender.
Musiala continued his run but his teammate Niclas Füllkrug got to the ball first. Füllkrug took one big touch forward, closing in on the top of the 6-yard box, and lined up to strike the ball.
Füllkrug thundered a shot into the top of the net, evening the score at 1-1.

Germany is last in its group, but this draw has increased its likelihood of advancing to the round of 16. Its simplest path of moving to the knockout stages is beating Costa Rica and Spain defeating Japan.

methodology: The 3-D scene was created using photographs and videos of the venue for reference. The positions of the players were determined from a photograph taken at that moment during the play. The player models were created using a machine learning model called ICON, which was created by Yuliang Xiu, Jinlong Yang, Dimitrios Tzionas, Michael J. Black and the Max Planck Institute. The models were refined manually using photographs as reference.
